title: Formula limitation with Timeline columns in multi-level boards (baseline vs current schedule calculation)
excerpt:

posts.author: Projects_Kick (Projects)
posts.cookedHtml:
I’m working with a client using the multi-level board structure and ran into a limitation with the Timeline column and the Formula column.

Their setup is:

  • A project board created from a template

  • Each item has two timeline columns:

    • Timeline (current schedule)

    • Baseline Timeline (created later as a snapshot baseline)

  • When a baseline timeline is added, there’s a ‘Difference’ column that comes with it by default. It calculates the difference between the end date of the baseline timeline and the end date of the current timeline. It uses the formula:
    DAYS({Baseline Timeline#End}, {Timeline#End})

However, in multi-level boards, the Timeline column and the baseline column are not supported in the Formula column, so {Timeline#End} and {Baseline Timeline#End} cannot be referenced.

Because each project board is created from a template and the baseline is only created later, we are trying to avoid solutions that require:

  • manually adding helper columns per board

  • manually adding automations per board

  • external integrations just to read timeline dates per board

Our goal is a scalable template-based solution that works across many project boards.

Questions:

  1. Is there any supported way to reference Timeline start/end dates in formulas on multi-level boards?

  2. Is there a recommended approach from monday for calculating baseline vs current schedule variance in this structure?

  3. Are there any upcoming improvements planned for Formula support with Timeline columns in multi-level boards?

Any guidance or recommended architecture for this use case would be helpful.

Thanks.

posts.author: YOGESHP (Yogesh Pokale)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @Projects_Kick

You can turn off the roll-up on the Timeline column, and then it will allow you to reference the Timeline’s start and end date values in the Formula column on multi-level boards.

posts.author: drtanvisachar (Dr. Tanvi Sachar- Certified Monday.com Partner)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hey @Projects_Kick !

At the moment, timeline and baseline timeline columns can’t be referenced inside the Formula column on multi-level boards. Because of that limitation, formulas like DAYS({Baseline Timeline#End}, {Timeline#End}) won’t work in that structure.

There isn’t currently a built-in way to calculate the variance between two timeline columns directly within a multi-level board using formulas. Most workarounds involve helper date columns that store the start or end dates and then referencing those in a formula, but that does require adding columns to the board structure.

If you’re trying to keep the template scalable, one approach is including the helper columns directly in the template so every board created from it already has the structure in place. That avoids needing to manually configure each board later.

For anything more dynamic, it usually requires automations or integrations that copy the timeline start/end values into standard date columns first, which the Formula column can then reference.

Questions about future support for timeline references in formulas are best directed to the monday.com product or support team, since changes to column compatibility or roadmap plans aren’t something the community can confirm. Sharing the use case with them can also help highlight the need for better support in multi-level boards.

posts.cookedHtml: I tried that already but the ‘Baseline’ timeline column becomes unsupported in the Gantt baseline view whenever I do that.

posts.author: MsquareAutomation (Msquare Automation Solutions)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @Projects_Kick

You’ve identified a real platform limitation — currently, Timeline columns (including Baseline timelines) are not supported in Formula columns on multi-level boards, so referencing {Timeline#Start} or {Timeline#End} isn’t possible natively.

There isn’t a scalable built-in workaround that avoids manual setup per board at this time.

However, this can be handled cleanly using Make Automation

Recommended scalable approach

Create a Make scenario that runs automatically when timeline values change:

  • Read the Timeline (current) start/end dates

  • Read the Baseline Timeline start/end dates

  • Calculate the variance (e.g., days difference)

  • Write the result back to a Number or Status column (e.g., “Schedule Variance”)

Because this runs at the template level (via board selection, naming pattern, or workspace scope), it works across all project boards without manual formulas or helper columns.
